turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Sotomayor has the opinion of the Court this morning in Case 22-666, Wilkinson versus Garland.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Immigration judges have statutory power to cancel the removal of a noncitizen and instead permit him to remain in the country lawfully. Statutory criteria govern whether a noncitizen is eligible for cancellation of removal. One criterion requires that the noncitizen establish that his removal from the United States would result in exceptional and extremely unusual hardship to his U.S. citizen or permanent resident spouse, parent or child. Petitioner Situ Kamu Wilkinson applied for cancellation of removal based in part on hardship to his 7-year-old U.S. citizen son, M. M suffers from a serious medical condition and relied on Wilkinson for emotional and financial support. Nevertheless, considering all the hardship factors in the aggregate, the immigration judge held that M’s situation did not meet the statutory standard for exceptional and extremely unusual hardship. The BIA affirmed, the Third Circuit dismissed Wilkinson’s position, holding that it lacked jurisdiction to review the immigration judge’s discretionary hardship determination. The Third Circuit was correct that 8 U.S.C. Code Section 1252(a)(2)(B)(1) strips Federal Courts of jurisdiction to review discretionary judgments on cancellation of removal. Section 1252(a)(2)(D) however restores jurisdiction to review questions of laws. In Guerrero-Lasprilla versus Barr, this Court held that the phrase, questions of law in that jurisdiction restoring provision includes mixed questions of law and fact. The application of the equitable tolling standard to a given set of facts was a mixed question reviewable as a question of law under that provision. Guerrero-Lasprilla compels the result in this case. The application of the statutory exceptional and extremely unusual hardship standard to a given set of facts is also inevitably a mixed question reviewable as a question of law under Section 1252(a)(2)(D). This hardship standard concededly requires a close examination of the facts. In this case the immigration judge had to examine whether M would suffer hardship that is beyond that which would ordinarily be expected to result from a father’s removal from the country. But just because a mixed question may require a court to immerse itself in facts does not transform a mixed question into a factual one. The factual findings themselves however, such as the immigration judge’s finding here that Wilkinson was credible and that his son suffers from a serious medical condition of course remain unreviewable. For these and other reasons set forth in the opinion, the Court reverses and vacates in part and remands for further proceedings consistent with this opinion. Justice Jackson has filed an opinion concurring in the judgment. Chief Justice Roberts has filed a dissenting opinion. Justice Alito has also filed a dissenting opinion in which Chief Justice Roberts and Justice Thomas have joined.
